title: Gynaecomastia
oneLiner: Benign proliferation of male breast glandular tissue; common, usually self-limiting, but may signal underlying pathology (e.g. testicular tumour, liver failure) or drug adverse effect.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Male breast cancer** – rare, but the single finding that says “this is not routine gynaecomastia”: **hard, irregular, fixed mass** with overlying skin puckering, nipple retraction, or bloody discharge. Any asymmetry or eccentric location (areolar ≠ central) is suspicious.
- **Testicular germ cell tumour (paraneoplastic gynaecomastia)** – the finding that says “this is not benign”: **palpable testicular mass** (often painless) or undescended testis on exam. hCG-producing tumours can present with gynaecomastia before a testicular lump is noticed.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
    - Bilateral or unilateral breast enlargement (patients often present with >1 month of swelling)
    - Tenderness or discomfort (common in early gynaecomastia; less so in breast cancer)
    - Rare: milky discharge (galactorrhoea — suggests prolactinoma or drugs)
    - Ask about: drug history (spironolactone, cimetidine, finasteride, anabolic steroids, cannabis, opioids, highly active antiretroviral therapy [HAART]), liver disease, hyperthyroidism, recent weight change, illicit drug use
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Palpate breast tissue: concentric, firm, rubbery mass **under** the areola (not hard/fixed). Tenderness points to active glandular proliferation.
    - Assess for pseudogynaecomastia (lipomastia) — diffuse fat without glandular ridge.
    - Look for signs of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia itself in cirrhosis).
    - Examine **testes** in every case (size, consistency, masses) and check for signs of hypogonadism (small testes, reduced facial/axillary hair, reduced muscle mass).
    - Check thyroid: tremor, goitre, eye signs.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Physiologic gynaecomastia** (neonatal, pubertal, senescent) – bilateral, tender, resolves spontaneously
- **Drug-induced** (spironolactone, cimetidine, finasteride, anabolic steroids, cannabis, anti-androgens for prostate cancer) – onset correlates with drug start, often bilateral
- **Liver disease** (cirrhosis) – bilateral, with other stigmata
- **Renal failure / dialysis** – bilateral, with uraemic symptoms
- **Hyperthyroidism** – bilateral, with thyrotoxic features (weight loss, tachycardia, tremor)
- **Hypogonadism** (primary or secondary) – bilateral, with small testes and low testosterone
- **Prolactinoma** – galactorrhoea, headache, visual field defects
- **Testicular or extragonadal germ cell tumour** – unilateral or bilateral, may be tender; examine testes, check hCG
- **Breast cancer** – unilateral, hard, non-tender, fixed, skin changes, lymphadenopathy
- **Lipomastia** (pseudogynaecomastia) – diffuse fatty enlargement without glandular disk
- **Splitter:** Unilateral, hard, eccentric mass + skin changes = breast cancer. Bilateral, symmetric, tender, sub-areolar disk = gynaecomastia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** none directly; use tape measure to document breast diameter (cm).
- **Bloods:**
    - LFTs (albumin, PT – chronic liver disease)
    - U&E, creatinine (renal failure)
    - TFTs (TSH, free T4)
    - LH, FSH, total testosterone (± calculated free testosterone or SHBG)
    - Oestradiol (if clinically indicated: hypogonadism, oestrogen-secreting tumour)
    - Prolactin (if galactorrhoea, hypogonadism, or visual symptoms)
    - β-hCG (in all with testicular mass or unexplained gynaecomastia; thresholds vary, but any detectable level requires urgent USS)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** **Breast ultrasound** – all unilateral or suspicious cases. Differentiates glandular vs fatty tissue; identifies breast mass.
    - **Second-line:** **Mammography** – reserved for older men (>40) or when ultrasound inconclusive/suspicious for cancer.
    - **Testicular ultrasound** – if testicular mass palpated, or hCG elevated, or bilateral/unilateral gynaecomastia unexplained.
    - **Chest X-ray** – if suspect lung cancer (paraneoplastic hCG).
    - **CT/MRI pituitary** – if prolactin markedly elevated or visual field defects.
- **Special tests:** Biopsy (core needle or excision) if imaging suggests malignancy.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system. Clinical diagnosis based on palpable glandular tissue ≥2 cm diameter.
- **Never-miss test:** **Testicular examination + (if indicated) testicular ultrasound** — omission can miss a curable testicular tumour.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Reassure patient. No specific emergency treatment unless testicular torsion or acute pain (rare).
    - Refer urgent testicular USS and urology if testicular mass or raised hCG.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Address underlying cause (e.g., stop offending drug if safe, treat hyperthyroidism, manage liver disease).
    - If drug-induced: discuss alternative with pharmacist/consultant before stopping essential drugs (e.g., spironolactone in cirrhosis).
    - Provide analgesia (paracetamol) if tender.
    - Consider tamoxifen or danazol only after senior/specialist review (off-label use).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - **Physiological** (pubertal/senile): reassurance, follow up in 6 months; 90% resolve spontaneously.
    - **Drug-induced:** resolution over weeks to months after withdrawal.
    - **Idiopathic, persistent, or painful:** refer to endocrinology or breast clinic. Options: tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 3–6 months (off-label) or danazol [check local policy]. Surgery (subcutaneous mastectomy, liposuction) if >12 months, >4 cm, or psychological distress.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A unless admitted for underlying disease (e.g., cirrhosis, hyperthyroidism). If no other indication, do not admit solely for gynaecomastia.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Tamoxifen** (off-label): 20 mg oral once daily for 3–6 months. Not first-line; only after specialist discussion.
- **Danazol** (off-label): 200–400 mg/day orally in divided doses. High side-effect profile (weight gain, hirsutism, liver toxicity). [Check local policy] for dose.
- **Key contraindications:** Tamoxifen increases VTE risk – avoid in previous DVT/PE, immobilisation, or thrombophilia. Danazol is teratogenic (contraindicated in women; use with care in men).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustments for short courses. Both caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Tamoxifen prescribed without excluding malignancy** – delay in diagnosis of breast cancer. Always image before starting treatment.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Tender gynaecomastia – self-limiting. Rare: haematoma/seroma post biopsy.
- **Chronic:** Psychological distress, body image issues. Rare: progression to breast cancer (if underlying male breast cancer misdiagnosed as gynaecomastia).
sections.Prognosis: - Pubertal gynaecomastia resolves spontaneously in 90% within 1–2 years. Drug-induced resolves 3–12 months after withdrawal. Idiopathic/persistent cases may require surgery. Underlying malignancy (testicular, breast) prognosis depends on stage.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine testes** — a painless testicular mass can be the only sign of a germ cell tumour, which is curable with early treatment.
- **Document drug history thoroughly** – include over-the-counter, anabolic steroids, and herbal supplements (e.g., lavender, tea tree oil have weak oestrogenic effects).
- **Use ultrasound (not mammography) first** in men under 40 – less radiation, better tissue characterisation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Biopsying without imaging** – may miss malignancy or cause haematoma. Always USS first.
- **Assuming it’s “just aging”** – elderly men can develop breast cancer; do not dismiss unilateral or progressive enlargement.
- **Using tamoxifen without checking baseline LFTs and renal function** – although not mandatory, avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** unilateral, enlarging, hard or fixed mass; new nipple inversion, ulceration, or bloody discharge; testicular pain or lump; new headache or visual disturbance (suggests pituitary lesion).
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 6 weeks if no cause identified; refer to breast clinic (2-week wait) if any suspicious feature. Endocrine referral if abnormal prolactin, testosterone, or pubertal delay.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / referral to breast/endocrine team** if:
    - Suspicious features on imaging (BI-RADS 4 or 5)
    - Raised β-hCG (suspect germ cell tumour – refer urology)
    - Prolactin >5000 mU/L or headache/visual field loss (suspect macroprolactinoma – refer endocrinology)
    - Testicular mass on exam or USS (refer urology urgent)
- **No specific NEWS2 thresholds** for gynaecomastia itself — escalate if underlying condition deteriorates.

rawMarkdown:
# Gynaecomastia

**One-liner:** Benign proliferation of male breast glandular tissue; common, usually self-limiting, but may signal underlying pathology (e.g. testicular tumour, liver failure) or drug adverse effect.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Male breast cancer** – rare, but the single finding that says “this is not routine gynaecomastia”: **hard, irregular, fixed mass** with overlying skin puckering, nipple retraction, or bloody discharge. Any asymmetry or eccentric location (areolar ≠ central) is suspicious.
- **Testicular germ cell tumour (paraneoplastic gynaecomastia)** – the finding that says “this is not benign”: **palpable testicular mass** (often painless) or undescended testis on exam. hCG-producing tumours can present with gynaecomastia before a testicular lump is noticed.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
    - Bilateral or unilateral breast enlargement (patients often present with >1 month of swelling)
    - Tenderness or discomfort (common in early gynaecomastia; less so in breast cancer)
    - Rare: milky discharge (galactorrhoea — suggests prolactinoma or drugs)
    - Ask about: drug history (spironolactone, cimetidine, finasteride, anabolic steroids, cannabis, opioids, highly active antiretroviral therapy [HAART]), liver disease, hyperthyroidism, recent weight change, illicit drug use
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Palpate breast tissue: concentric, firm, rubbery mass **under** the areola (not hard/fixed). Tenderness points to active glandular proliferation.
    - Assess for pseudogynaecomastia (lipomastia) — diffuse fat without glandular ridge.
    - Look for signs of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia itself in cirrhosis).
    - Examine **testes** in every case (size, consistency, masses) and check for signs of hypogonadism (small testes, reduced facial/axillary hair, reduced muscle mass).
    - Check thyroid: tremor, goitre, eye signs.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Physiologic gynaecomastia** (neonatal, pubertal, senescent) – bilateral, tender, resolves spontaneously
- **Drug-induced** (spironolactone, cimetidine, finasteride, anabolic steroids, cannabis, anti-androgens for prostate cancer) – onset correlates with drug start, often bilateral
- **Liver disease** (cirrhosis) – bilateral, with other stigmata
- **Renal failure / dialysis** – bilateral, with uraemic symptoms
- **Hyperthyroidism** – bilateral, with thyrotoxic features (weight loss, tachycardia, tremor)
- **Hypogonadism** (primary or secondary) – bilateral, with small testes and low testosterone
- **Prolactinoma** – galactorrhoea, headache, visual field defects
- **Testicular or extragonadal germ cell tumour** – unilateral or bilateral, may be tender; examine testes, check hCG
- **Breast cancer** – unilateral, hard, non-tender, fixed, skin changes, lymphadenopathy
- **Lipomastia** (pseudogynaecomastia) – diffuse fatty enlargement without glandular disk
- **Splitter:** Unilateral, hard, eccentric mass + skin changes = breast cancer. Bilateral, symmetric, tender, sub-areolar disk = gynaecomastia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** none directly; use tape measure to document breast diameter (cm).
- **Bloods:**
    - LFTs (albumin, PT – chronic liver disease)
    - U&E, creatinine (renal failure)
    - TFTs (TSH, free T4)
    - LH, FSH, total testosterone (± calculated free testosterone or SHBG)
    - Oestradiol (if clinically indicated: hypogonadism, oestrogen-secreting tumour)
    - Prolactin (if galactorrhoea, hypogonadism, or visual symptoms)
    - β-hCG (in all with testicular mass or unexplained gynaecomastia; thresholds vary, but any detectable level requires urgent USS)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** **Breast ultrasound** – all unilateral or suspicious cases. Differentiates glandular vs fatty tissue; identifies breast mass.
    - **Second-line:** **Mammography** – reserved for older men (>40) or when ultrasound inconclusive/suspicious for cancer.
    - **Testicular ultrasound** – if testicular mass palpated, or hCG elevated, or bilateral/unilateral gynaecomastia unexplained.
    - **Chest X-ray** – if suspect lung cancer (paraneoplastic hCG).
    - **CT/MRI pituitary** – if prolactin markedly elevated or visual field defects.
- **Special tests:** Biopsy (core needle or excision) if imaging suggests malignancy.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system. Clinical diagnosis based on palpable glandular tissue ≥2 cm diameter.
- **Never-miss test:** **Testicular examination + (if indicated) testicular ultrasound** — omission can miss a curable testicular tumour.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Reassure patient. No specific emergency treatment unless testicular torsion or acute pain (rare).
    - Refer urgent testicular USS and urology if testicular mass or raised hCG.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Address underlying cause (e.g., stop offending drug if safe, treat hyperthyroidism, manage liver disease).
    - If drug-induced: discuss alternative with pharmacist/consultant before stopping essential drugs (e.g., spironolactone in cirrhosis).
    - Provide analgesia (paracetamol) if tender.
    - Consider tamoxifen or danazol only after senior/specialist review (off-label use).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - **Physiological** (pubertal/senile): reassurance, follow up in 6 months; 90% resolve spontaneously.
    - **Drug-induced:** resolution over weeks to months after withdrawal.
    - **Idiopathic, persistent, or painful:** refer to endocrinology or breast clinic. Options: tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 3–6 months (off-label) or danazol [check local policy]. Surgery (subcutaneous mastectomy, liposuction) if >12 months, >4 cm, or psychological distress.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A unless admitted for underlying disease (e.g., cirrhosis, hyperthyroidism). If no other indication, do not admit solely for gynaecomastia.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Tamoxifen** (off-label): 20 mg oral once daily for 3–6 months. Not first-line; only after specialist discussion.
- **Danazol** (off-label): 200–400 mg/day orally in divided doses. High side-effect profile (weight gain, hirsutism, liver toxicity). [Check local policy] for dose.
- **Key contraindications:** Tamoxifen increases VTE risk – avoid in previous DVT/PE, immobilisation, or thrombophilia. Danazol is teratogenic (contraindicated in women; use with care in men).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustments for short courses. Both caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Tamoxifen prescribed without excluding malignancy** – delay in diagnosis of breast cancer. Always image before starting treatment.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Tender gynaecomastia – self-limiting. Rare: haematoma/seroma post biopsy.
- **Chronic:** Psychological distress, body image issues. Rare: progression to breast cancer (if underlying male breast cancer misdiagnosed as gynaecomastia).

### Prognosis
- Pubertal gynaecomastia resolves spontaneously in 90% within 1–2 years. Drug-induced resolves 3–12 months after withdrawal. Idiopathic/persistent cases may require surgery. Underlying malignancy (testicular, breast) prognosis depends on stage.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always examine testes** — a painless testicular mass can be the only sign of a germ cell tumour, which is curable with early treatment.
- **Document drug history thoroughly** – include over-the-counter, anabolic steroids, and herbal supplements (e.g., lavender, tea tree oil have weak oestrogenic effects).
- **Use ultrasound (not mammography) first** in men under 40 – less radiation, better tissue characterisation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Biopsying without imaging** – may miss malignancy or cause haematoma. Always USS first.
- **Assuming it’s “just aging”** – elderly men can develop breast cancer; do not dismiss unilateral or progressive enlargement.
- **Using tamoxifen without checking baseline LFTs and renal function** – although not mandatory, avoid in severe hepatic impairment.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** unilateral, enlarging, hard or fixed mass; new nipple inversion, ulceration, or bloody discharge; testicular pain or lump; new headache or visual disturbance (suggests pituitary lesion).
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 6 weeks if no cause identified; refer to breast clinic (2-week wait) if any suspicious feature. Endocrine referral if abnormal prolactin, testosterone, or pubertal delay.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / referral to breast/endocrine team** if:
    - Suspicious features on imaging (BI-RADS 4 or 5)
    - Raised β-hCG (suspect germ cell tumour – refer urology)
    - Prolactin >5000 mU/L or headache/visual field loss (suspect macroprolactinoma – refer endocrinology)
    - Testicular mass on exam or USS (refer urology urgent)
- **No specific NEWS2 thresholds** for gynaecomastia itself — escalate if underlying condition deteriorates.
